personas.profile:
Maya Chen is a 34-year-old female product manager in the United States who works full-time and spends 7–8 hours online most days; she feels mentally tired after long sessions, often regrets time spent online, and is especially sensitive to social media, images, and news that leave her feeling disconnected or misled.

Her end goals are to cut mindless scrolling so she can use digital time for meaningful tasks, to quickly verify whether an online claim or post is trustworthy before acting on it, and to step away from digital overload before it drains her energy and focus. These goals matter because she wants her online habits to support work, family, and practical responsibilities instead of replacing them.

Her life goals are to feel in control of her attention and to protect her wellbeing by making her digital life more intentional and less draining. She is also motivated by wanting to stay informed without becoming cynical or overwhelmed.

Her experience goals are to feel reassured that she is making sound decisions, to feel less guilty about how she spends time online, and to feel that the product helps her regain clarity rather than adding more noise. She wants the interaction to feel efficient, trustworthy, and non-judgmental.

Her key behaviors are that she often checks multiple sources when something seems questionable, because this supports her goal of verifying trustworthiness; she relies most on academics or experts and clear facts/evidence, because that helps her decide what to believe; she usually takes a break when digital life feels overwhelming, because she is trying to protect her energy; and she is likely to use the product to reduce passive scrolling and replace it with more purposeful activity, because she wants better use of her time.

In a typical scenario of use, Maya opens the digital life coaching service after noticing she has lost an hour to social media during a work break and feels drained and guilty. She asks the product to help her figure out whether a news post she saw is reliable and to suggest a better way to spend the rest of her break. The service surfaces a fact-checking path based on multiple sources and expert-backed evidence, then recommends a short reset so she can return to work with more focus. Maya leaves feeling more in control, less misled, and better able to use the rest of her day intentionally.

Daniel Brooks is a 46-year-old male freelance consultant in the United Kingdom who works long hours online, often 9–12 hours a day; he uses digital tools for work and information, but after extended use he feels tired, frustrated, and increasingly wary of social media, news feeds, and AI-generated content.

His end goals are to reduce the time he loses to algorithm-driven feeds, to identify whether content is human-made or AI-generated before he relies on it, and to find trustworthy, evidence-based information without wasting time searching. These goals are tied to his need to stay productive and avoid being manipulated by misleading content.

His life goals are to maintain a healthy work-life boundary and to preserve his ability to think clearly in a digital environment that feels increasingly crowded and artificial. He also wants to stay useful and informed without becoming dependent on tools he does not trust.

His experience goals are to feel confident that the information he is using is real and relevant, to feel less irritated by digital distractions, and to feel that he can switch off without falling behind. He wants the product to make him feel supported, not surveilled or pressured.

His key behaviors are that he often looks for multiple sources before believing something online, because this aligns with his goal of verifying evidence; he trusts academics and experts more than social media, because that helps him filter out low-quality content; he is likely to stop and take a break when overwhelmed, because he is trying to protect his focus and energy; and he is particularly attentive to social media, images, and videos, because those are the content types he distrusts most and wants help evaluating.

In a realistic scenario of use, Daniel is preparing for a client presentation and finds a viral post claiming a new AI tool will replace most of his industry. He opens the digital life coaching service to check whether the claim is credible and whether the source has bias. The product guides him toward expert-backed evidence and a quick comparison of sources, then suggests a short break so he can reset before continuing work. Daniel closes the session feeling more grounded, less manipulated, and better able to separate useful information from digital noise.
